learn.title: Averages
learn.intro: Almost every average problem reduces to the same trick: convert averages into sums, do simple arithmetic on the sums, then convert back to an average at the very end. Master that one move and every variant below becomes easy.

sections.explanation: Average = Sum of all values ÷ Number of values. It represents the 'leveled out' value if all the quantities were made equal.
sections.realLifeExample: If you score 80, 90 and 100 in three tests, your average score is (80+90+100)/3 = 90 — the score you'd have gotten if all three tests carried equal, balanced performance.
sections.memoryTrick: Average = total pooled together, then shared out equally.
sections.shortcutTrick: Use the 'deviation from an assumed average' method: pick a round number close to the values, find how far each value deviates from it, average the deviations, then add back to the assumed average. For 48, 52, 55, 61: assume avg=50; deviations −2, 2, 5, 11 sum to 16, average deviation 4; actual average = 50+4 = 54.
formulaCard: Average = Sum of observations / Number of observations
Sum of observations = Average × Number of observations
commonMistakes: Dividing by the wrong count — for example forgetting one value in the list.
Confusing average with median or mode.
sections.talentSprintTip: TalentSprint often tests basic averages with a twist: 'the average is X, one value is removed, find the new average' — always go back to the sum rather than trying to shortcut without it.
sections.tcsNqtTip: TCS NQT rewards the deviation method for double- and triple-digit numbers — it's faster than direct addition under time pressure.

sections.explanation: When groups of different sizes are combined, the overall average isn't the simple average of the group averages — it's the weighted average, using each group's size as its 'weight'.
sections.realLifeExample: If 30 students average 70 marks and 20 students average 90 marks, the combined average isn't (70+90)/2=80; it's the weighted average using group sizes: (30×70+20×90)/50 = 78.
sections.memoryTrick: Bigger groups pull the average harder toward themselves.
sections.shortcutTrick: Weighted average = (w1a1+w2a2+...)/(w1+w2+...). If you only need to know which side of the simple average the answer lies, compare group sizes — the larger group's average dominates.
commonMistakes: Averaging the group averages directly, ignoring the group sizes.
Using the wrong weight for a group — swapping which weight belongs to which average.
sections.talentSprintTip: TalentSprint's weighted average questions almost always describe two batches or classes explicitly with sizes — extract w and a for each group before doing any arithmetic.
sections.tcsNqtTip: TCS NQT often frames weighted average as a 'mixture' of marks, products, or investments across unequal batches — treat it exactly like the standard weighted average formula.

sections.explanation: This is about combining or splitting averages of subgroups within a total — for example, finding the average of a subset given the average of the whole and the average of the remaining part.
sections.realLifeExample: If a class of 40 students has an average height of 150 cm, and the 15 tallest students average 160 cm, you can find the average height of the remaining 25 students using the total-sum method.
sections.memoryTrick: Total sum is the anchor — always convert averages back into sums, combine or subtract, then divide again.
sections.shortcutTrick: Sum of the whole group = average × count. Sum of the remaining group = total sum − sum of the known subgroup. Divide by the remaining count to get their average.
commonMistakes: Trying to subtract averages directly instead of converting to sums first.
Using the wrong count for the remaining group — forgetting to subtract the subgroup count from the total count.
sections.talentSprintTip: TalentSprint often layers this with a second condition (like 'if 5 more students join with average X') — finish the base case completely before layering the next change.
sections.tcsNqtTip: TCS NQT versions frequently involve 'excluding the highest and lowest values' — always subtract exactly 2 from the count when both extremes are excluded.

sections.explanation: When one or more members of a group are replaced by new members, the average changes by a predictable amount tied to the difference between the old and new values.
sections.realLifeExample: A cricket team's average age changes when one player is replaced by a younger substitute — the age difference, spread across the whole team, shifts the average.
sections.memoryTrick: Replacement shifts the average by (difference in the replaced value) ÷ (group size).
sections.shortcutTrick: New average = old average + (new value − old value)/count. This avoids recomputing the whole sum from scratch.
formulaCard: Change in average = (New value − Old value) / Number of items
New sum = Old sum − Old value(s) removed + New value(s) added
commonMistakes: Forgetting to divide the value difference by the group size — the shift is spread across everyone, not applied directly.
Mixing up the sign of the difference, i.e. whether the average increases or decreases.
sections.talentSprintTip: TalentSprint often asks 'by how much does the average change' rather than the new average directly — that's a direct application of the change formula, with no need to find the new average at all.
sections.tcsNqtTip: TCS NQT versions frequently replace multiple people at once — compute the total value swapped in and out first, then divide by the group size, rather than handling replacements one at a time.

sections.explanation: Average age problems track how a group's average age evolves over time, with people joining or leaving, or simply with birthdays — every year, everyone in the group ages by 1 year, so the average shifts naturally.
sections.realLifeExample: A family of 5 has an average age of 25 years today. In 4 years, everyone will be 4 years older, so the average age will simply be 25+4=29, as long as no one joins or leaves.
sections.memoryTrick: Everyone ages together — average age moves in lockstep with time, unless someone enters or exits.
sections.shortcutTrick: If n years pass with no change in membership, new average = old average + n. If a person joins or leaves, handle it like a replacement/group-average problem, but remember to also add the elapsed years for everyone else first.
formulaCard: Average age after n years (same members) = Current average age + n
When membership changes: age the existing members by the elapsed years first, then add or remove the new/old member's age separately
commonMistakes: Forgetting to account for elapsed years for members already in the group when a new member joins later.
Applying the '+n years' shift after already handling a birth or departure, causing double-counting.
sections.talentSprintTip: TalentSprint mixes 'years later' with 'a member leaves/joins' in the same question — always separate the two effects: first age everyone by n years, then apply any membership change.
sections.tcsNqtTip: TCS NQT's family/age puzzles frequently need a timeline set up year by year rather than a single shortcut formula — write out each stage explicitly.

sections.explanation: 'Mixed average' problems combine two or more of the above types (weighted, replacement, group-based) in a single scenario, often across multiple steps — the key skill is breaking the problem into separate average-formula applications, solved in sequence.
sections.realLifeExample: A shop's average daily sales for the first 20 days of a month is ₹5,000. For the whole month (30 days) to average ₹5,500, the last 10 days need a specific, higher average — working this out requires two average-to-sum conversions before comparing.
sections.memoryTrick: When it feels like several average problems glued together, solve them exactly like that — one small step at a time.
sections.shortcutTrick: Convert every stated average into a sum first (average×count=sum). Add or subtract sums and counts algebraically, and convert back to an average only at the very last step.
formulaCard: Break every stated average into Sum = Average × Count before combining scenarios
Combine sums and counts separately, then divide once at the very end
commonMistakes: Trying to average multiple averages directly without weighting by count.
Losing track of which count belongs to which sum partway through a multi-step problem.
sections.talentSprintTip: TalentSprint's hardest average questions are almost always 'mixed' — expect 2-3 average-to-sum conversions chained together; write each sum down explicitly rather than doing it mentally.
sections.tcsNqtTip: TCS NQT's mixed average problems often hide inside 'target average' questions ('what should the next value be to reach a target average') — solve for the required total sum first, then subtract the sum so far.

solvedExamples.question: The average of 5 numbers is 27. If one number is excluded, the average of the remaining 4 numbers becomes 25. Find the excluded number.
solvedExamples.solution:
Sum of all 5 numbers = 27×5 = 135.
Sum of the remaining 4 numbers = 25×4 = 100.
Excluded number = 135 − 100 = 35.

solvedExamples.question: In a class, 30 students have an average score of 70 and 20 students have an average score of 90. Find the average score of the entire class of 50 students.
The average weight of 10 boys is 42 kg. One boy weighing 35 kg leaves and is replaced by a new boy, after which the average becomes 43 kg. Find the new boy's weight.
solvedExamples.solution:
Old sum = 42×10 = 420.
New sum = 43×10 = 430.
New boy's weight = old boy's weight + (new sum − old sum) = 35 + (430−420) = 35+10 = 45 kg.

solvedExamples.question: The average age of a family of 4 members is 24 years today. Find their average age after 5 years, assuming no one joins or leaves.
solvedExamples.solution: With no change in membership, the average simply shifts by the elapsed years: 24 + 5 = 29.

solvedExamples.question: The average of 8 numbers is 15. The average of the first 5 numbers is 12. Find the average of the remaining 3 numbers.
solvedExamples.solution:
Total sum = 15×8 = 120.
Sum of first 5 = 12×5 = 60.
Sum of remaining 3 = 120 − 60 = 60.
Average of remaining 3 = 60/3 = 20.

practiceExamples.question: The average of 6 numbers is 18. If one number is removed, the average of the remaining 5 becomes 16. Find the removed number.
practiceExamples.hint: Sum of 6 = 108, sum of remaining 5 = 80, removed number = 108 − 80.

practiceExamples.question: 25 students average 60 marks and 15 students average 80 marks. Find the combined average of all 40 students.
The average score of a class of 30 is 65. The average score of 18 of them is 70. Find the average score of the remaining 12.
practiceExamples.hint: Total sum = 1950, known sum = 1260, remaining sum = 690, divide by 12.

practiceExamples.question: The average age of 8 friends is 22 years. One friend aged 26 leaves and is replaced by a new friend, and the new average becomes 21 years. Find the new friend's age.
The average age of a group of 5 people is 30 years today. Find their average age 6 years from now, assuming the group is unchanged.
practiceExamples.hint: No membership change → new average = old average + elapsed years.

easy.question: Find the average of 10, 20 and 30.
whyWrong: 30 is just the largest of the three numbers, not their average.
15 is half of 30, not the actual average.
easy.shortcut: Add all values, then divide by the count — the direct definition works fine for small sets.

easy.question: The average of 5 numbers is 20. Find their sum.
whyWrong: 20 is just the average itself, not the total sum.
4 doesn't relate to the calculation at all.
easy.shortcut: Whenever a sum is needed but not given directly, compute Average × Count first.

easy.question: The average weight of 4 people is 60 kg. If a fifth person weighing 75 kg joins, find the new average.
easy.explanation: Old sum = 240. Adding the new person: 240+75=315. New average = 315/5 = 63.
whyWrong: 60 was the old average, unaffected by the new addition.
easy.shortcut: Convert the old average to a sum first, add the new value, then divide by the new count.

easy.question: In a class, 20 students average 50 marks and 10 students average 80 marks. Find the class's overall average.
whyWrong: 65 would be the simple average of 50 and 80, ignoring the different group sizes.
easy.shortcut: Never average the group averages directly when the group sizes differ — always weight by size.

easy.question: A family of 3 has an average age of 20 years. What will their average age be after 3 years, assuming no one joins or leaves?
easy.explanation: Every member ages by the same 3 years, so the average simply increases by 3: 20+3=23.
whyWrong: 20 was the average today, not 3 years later.
easy.shortcut: With no membership change, average age after n years = current average + n.

medium.question: The average of 7 numbers is 24. If the average of the first 4 numbers is 21, find the average of the remaining 3.
medium.explanation: Total sum = 24×7 = 168. Sum of first 4 = 21×4 = 84. Remaining 3 sum = 168−84 = 84. Their average = 84/3 = 28.
whyWrong: 24 is the overall average, not just the remaining group's.
medium.shortcut: Always convert every average to a sum first, subtract, then divide once at the end.

medium.question: The average marks of 24 students is 56. If the teacher's marks (80) are also included, find the new average of all 25 people.
whyWrong: 56 was the old average before the teacher's marks were added.
medium.shortcut: Adding one value shifts the average only slightly when the group is already large — estimate first, then verify.

medium.question: The average height of 12 boys is 150 cm. If the average height of 8 of them is 145 cm, find the average height of the remaining 4 boys.
medium.explanation: Total sum = 150×12 = 1800. Sum of 8 boys = 145×8 = 1160. Remaining 4 boys' sum = 1800−1160 = 640. Their average = 640/4 = 160.
whyWrong: 150 is the overall average, not the remaining group's.
medium.shortcut: Subtract the known sum from the total sum, then divide by the remaining count only.

medium.question: The average weight of 15 students is 48 kg. A student weighing 60 kg leaves and is replaced by a new student, after which the average becomes 48.8 kg. Find the new student's weight.
medium.explanation: Old sum = 48×15 = 720. New sum = 48.8×15 = 732. New student's weight = old value + (new sum − old sum) = 60 + 12 = 72.
medium.shortcut: New value = Old value replaced + (New total sum − Old total sum).

medium.question: The average age of a husband and wife was 25 years at marriage. After 5 years, they have a 1-year-old child. What is the family's average age now?
medium.explanation: At marriage, husband+wife sum=50. After 5 years, each ages by 5, adding 10 to the sum → 60. Including the 1-year-old child: sum=61, count=3. Average = 61/3 ≈ 20.33 years.
medium.shortcut: Age everyone already in the group by the elapsed years first, then add the new member's age separately.

medium.question: The average of 11 results is 50. The average of the first 6 is 49 and that of the last 6 is 52. Find the 6th result.
medium.explanation: Sum of all 11 = 550. Sum of first 6 = 294. Sum of last 6 = 312. Since 6+6=12 exceeds 11, the 6th result is counted in both groups: 6th = 294+312−550 = 56.
whyWrong: 50 is the overall average, not the double-counted term.
medium.shortcut: When two overlapping subgroup sums cover more terms than the total, the overlapping term = (sum of both subgroups) − (total sum).

medium.question: The average of the ages of a father and son is 28 years. The ratio of their ages is 5:2. Find the son's age.
medium.explanation: Sum of ages = 28×2 = 56. With ratio 5:2, let ages be 5x and 2x: 7x=56, x=8. Son's age = 2×8=16.
whyWrong: 14 doesn't satisfy the 5:2 ratio with sum 56.
medium.shortcut: Convert the average to a sum, then split it directly using the given ratio.

medium.question: A batsman's average after 16 innings is 39. In the 17th innings, he scores 90, increasing his average by 3. Find his new average.
medium.explanation: Old sum = 39×16 = 624. New sum = 624+90 = 714. New average = 714/17 = 42, which is indeed 3 more than 39.
whyWrong: 39 was the average before the 17th innings, not after.
medium.shortcut: Set up new average = old average + n, and solve using old sum + new score = new count × (old average + n).

medium.question: The average of 30 numbers is 40. If each number is multiplied by 3, find the new average.
medium.explanation: Multiplying every number by 3 multiplies the sum by 3, and since the count is unchanged, the average also multiplies by 3: 40×3=120.
whyWrong: 43 wrongly adds 3 instead of multiplying by 3.
medium.shortcut: Scaling every value by a constant k scales the average by the same constant k.

medium.question: The average of 20 numbers is 15. If 5 is added to each of the first 10 numbers, find the new average of all 20 numbers.
medium.explanation: Original sum = 15×20 = 300. Adding 5 to each of 10 numbers adds a total of 50 to the sum: new sum = 350. New average = 350/20 = 17.5.
medium.shortcut: When a change applies only to some numbers, add up the total change and divide by the full count, not just the affected count.

hard.question: The average weight of A, B and C is 45 kg. The average weight of A and B is 40 kg, and the average weight of B and C is 43 kg. Find the weight of B.
whyWrong: 35 doesn't satisfy all three given averages simultaneously.
40 is the average of A and B, not B's individual weight.
45 is the overall average, not B's individual weight.
hard.shortcut: Use the three given sums to isolate each individual value: total minus each pair sum gives the third person's value.

hard.question: The average of 5 consecutive even numbers is 34. Find the largest number.
hard.explanation: For consecutive even numbers, the average equals the middle term, so the middle number is 34. The largest of the five is 34+4=38.
whyWrong: 36 is only the 2nd-largest number, not the largest.
40 overshoots by miscounting the gap as 6 instead of 4.
hard.shortcut: For any evenly-spaced set (consecutive integers, evens, or odds), the average always equals the middle term.

hard.question: The average of 9 numbers is 50. The average of the first 4 is 54 and the average of the last 4 is 45. Find the 5th number.
hard.explanation: Total sum=450. Sum of first 4=216. Sum of last 4=180. Since 4+4+1=9 (no overlap), the 5th number = 450−216−180=54.
whyWrong: 50 is the overall average, not the 5th number specifically.
45 was the average of the last 4, not the 5th number.
hard.shortcut: Check whether the sub-groups overlap the total count before subtracting — here 4+4+1=9 confirms no overlap.

hard.question: A trader sells 20 items at an average price of ₹150 and 30 items at ₹200. He wants to sell 10 more items so the overall average of all 60 items becomes ₹190. Find the required average price of the last 10 items.
hard.explanation: Target total sum=190×60=11400. Sum so far=3000+6000=9000. Required sum for the last 10 items=11400−9000=2400. Average=2400/10=240.
hard.shortcut: For 'target average' questions, compute the total sum needed for the target first, then subtract what's already accounted for.

hard.question: The average age of a class of 20 students is 15 years. If the teacher's age is also included, the average increases by 1 year. Find the teacher's age.
hard.explanation: Old sum=20×15=300. New average with teacher included=16, new count=21, so new sum=21×16=336. Teacher's age=336−300=36.
hard.shortcut: Always recompute the new sum using the new count and new average — don't add the average increase directly to a person's age.

hard.question: The average of 6 numbers is 8. If 3 is subtracted from each of the first 3 numbers and 2 is added to each of the last 3, find the new average.
hard.explanation: Original sum=8×6=48. Net change=(−3×3)+(2×3)=−9+6=−3. New sum=45. New average=45/6=7.5.
whyWrong: 8 was the original average, unaffected by the changes described.
hard.shortcut: Compute the total net change to the sum first, not the average, then divide by the same count.

hard.question: A car covers the first half of a journey at 40 km/h and the second half at 60 km/h. Find its average speed for the whole journey.
hard.explanation: For equal distances at two different speeds, average speed = 2ab/(a+b) = (2×40×60)/(40+60) = 4800/100 = 48 km/h — not the simple average of 50.
whyWrong: 50 is the simple average of the two speeds, which is wrong for equal-distance journeys — the harmonic mean formula must be used instead.
hard.shortcut: For equal distances at two speeds, always use average speed = 2ab/(a+b), never the plain average of the two speeds.

hard.question: The average of 7 numbers is 20. One number was misread as 25 instead of its actual value 45. Find the correct average.
hard.explanation: Wrong sum=20×7=140. Correcting the misread number: 140−25+45=160. Correct average=160/7≈22.86.
hard.shortcut: For a 'misread value' correction, remove the wrong value and add the correct one to the sum, then divide again by the same count.

hard.question: The average of Rahul's marks in 5 subjects is 68. After his 6th subject's result is added, his average becomes 70. Find his mark in the 6th subject.
hard.explanation: Old sum=68×5=340. New sum=70×6=420. Sixth subject's mark=420−340=80.
hard.shortcut: Whenever a new value increases the average, it must be well above the new average itself — a good sanity check for your final answer.

hard.question: Section A has 24 students averaging 65, and Section B has 26 students averaging x. If the combined class of 50 students averages 70, find x.
hard.explanation: Combined sum=70×50=3500. Section A's sum=24×65=1560. Section B's sum=3500−1560=1940. Section B's average x=1940/26≈74.6.
whyWrong: 70 is the combined average, not Section B's own average.
hard.shortcut: Convert every average to a sum, isolate the unknown group's sum by subtraction, then divide by its own count only.

quickSheet: Average = Sum ÷ Count; Sum = Average × Count — convert between these constantly.
Weighted average = (w1a1+w2a2+...)/(sum of weights) — never average group averages directly if group sizes differ.
To find a subgroup's average: Sum(whole) − Sum(known part), divided by the remaining count.
Replacement average: New value = Old value replaced + (New total sum − Old total sum).
With no membership change, average age after n years = current average + n.
Equal-distance average speed = 2ab/(a+b), never the plain average of the two speeds.
oneMinute: Sum is the anchor — convert every average to a sum before combining or comparing.
Bigger groups pull a weighted average harder toward themselves.
Scaling every value by k scales the average by k too.
revision.summary: Every average problem, however it's dressed up — weighted, replacement, age, or mixed — reduces to the same two moves: convert averages to sums using Sum = Average × Count, combine or subtract those sums, then divide back into an average only at the very end. Resist the urge to average averages directly; always weight by count first.
